name: Iceland

outbound_official.note: No official Iceland government outbound visa-list page located. Iceland's Ministry for Foreign Affairs (government.is) and the island.is portal do not publish a static country-by-country list of where Icelandic ordinary-passport holders can travel without a prior consular visa. All government.is pages returned HTTP 402; exhaustive search of island.is, government.is, and utl.is found only inbound visa pages (for foreign nationals coming to Iceland). Unlike India's MEA (mea.gov.in/vffin), Iceland has no equivalent origin-side outbound travel authorization page. Rely on destination-side data.

visa_free.nationality: All EU member state nationals (EEA freedom of movement)
visa_free.notes: EU/EEA citizens have unlimited freedom of movement to live, work and reside in Iceland under the EEA Agreement. No visa required. May use national ID card in lieu of passport.
Fellow EFTA/EEA states. Citizens of Norway and Liechtenstein enjoy freedom of movement to Iceland under the EEA Agreement with no stay limit.
Switzerland participates in Schengen and has a bilateral agreement with the EU/EEA. Swiss nationals have free movement rights and no visa requirement for Iceland.

visa_free.nationality: Nordic countries (Denmark, Sweden, Finland, Norway) – Nordic Passport Union
visa_free.notes: Iceland is a founding member of the Nordic Passport Union (since 1 January 1966). Nordic citizens (Denmark, Finland, Norway, Sweden) may travel to Iceland without any identity documentation, with no stay limit.

Biometric passport required — non-biometric passport holders require a Schengen visa. Schengen Annex II visa-exempt (EU Regulation 2018/1806). 90 days in any 180-day period. ETIAS pre-travel authorization will also be required once ETIAS launches (expected Q4 2026, not yet in force as of June 2026).

visa_policy.notes: Iceland is a full Schengen Area member and an EEA/EFTA state. It applies the Schengen Common Visa Policy (EU Regulation 2018/1806). There is no Iceland-specific e-visa or visa-on-arrival scheme; a Schengen short-stay visa issued by any Schengen state is valid for Iceland. The official visa-checker tool at island.is/en/do-you-need-a-visa does not publish a static country list — it uses an interactive tool. Full Annex II country enumeration is based on EU Regulation 2018/1806 as applicable to Iceland per its Schengen association agreement. Vanuatu was removed from Annex II in December 2024 via Regulation (EU) 2025/11 and now requires a visa. The EU Entry/Exit System (EES) has been operational at Iceland's borders since 10 April 2026, requiring biometric registration of non-EEA/Schengen travelers on arrival.

credential.subtype: any valid Schengen visa (uniform visa or national long-stay visa)

conditional_access.conditions: Holders of a valid Schengen visa (uniform short-stay visa or national long-stay visa) issued by any Schengen member state do not need to apply for a separate Iceland visa. Stay subject to the 90/180-day Schengen rule. Visa must be valid.

credential.subtype: any valid residence permit issued by an EEA/EFTA country

conditional_access.conditions: Non-EEA/EFTA nationals who hold a valid residence permit issued by another EEA/EFTA country may enter Iceland without a separate Iceland visa, for up to 90 days in any 180-day period.

credential.subtype: EU family member residence card issued by any EU member state

conditional_access.conditions: Residence cards for family members of EU/EEA nationals issued by any EU member state qualify for entry to Iceland without a separate visa.

Nationals of Australia, Brazil, Canada, Chile, Israel, Japan, Malaysia, Mexico, New Zealand, South Korea, and Uruguay may stay in Iceland for up to 3 months beyond the standard 90-day Schengen allowance, for a total of approximately 180 days, under bilateral extended-stay agreements. Applies to bona fide non-immigrants only. Right is forfeited if a residence permit application is submitted during the stay. Cannot apply for a residence permit while in Iceland under this agreement.
Holders of diplomatic passports, special passports, and service passports issued by Turkey are exempt from the visa requirement for Iceland.
Holders of diplomatic passports and service passports issued by India are exempt from the visa requirement for Iceland. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.
Holders of diplomatic passports and service passports issued by Pakistan are exempt from the visa requirement for Iceland. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.
Holders of diplomatic passports and service passports issued by South Africa are exempt from the visa requirement for Iceland. Ordinary passport holders require a visa.
Holders of diplomatic passports issued by China are exempt from the visa requirement for Iceland. Service and ordinary passport holders require a visa.

cbi.notes: Iceland has no citizenship-by-investment (CBI) program. Icelandic citizenship is only acquired by birth, descent, adoption, or naturalisation through residence. General naturalisation requires 7 years of continuous domicile in Iceland. Shorter periods apply for: spouses married to an Icelandic citizen (4 years since marriage, Icelandic spouse must have been citizen for 5+ years), cohabiting partners of Icelandic citizens (5 years), Nordic citizens (4 years), children of Icelandic citizens (2 years), refugees/humanitarian permit holders (5 years), stateless persons (5 years). Dual citizenship is permitted since the 1 July 2003 amendment to the Nationality Act. Icelandic language proficiency is required for most paths. There is no investment or donation pathway to citizenship. Sources: island.is/en/electronic-application-for-icelandic-citizenship/when-can-I-apply, government.is/topics/foreign-nationals/citizenship/.

programs.name: Residence Permit for Qualified Professionals (Specialist Employees)
programs.notes: For specialist/expert employees. Requires an employment contract meeting statutory minimum wage, valid health insurance (min 6 months, ISK 2,000,000 coverage), criminal record certificate, and valid passport. Initial permit for 1 year, renewable for 2 years. Expedited processing available for qualified professionals (within 30 days if all documents submitted; standard is up to 180 days). Permanent residence possible after 4 continuous years. No investment threshold.

programs.name: Residence Permit for Employees (Labour Shortage)
programs.notes: For positions in labour shortage occupations. Same general requirements as specialist employee permit. No investment pathway.

programs.name: Residence Permit on Grounds of Legitimate and Special Purpose
programs.notes: Covers specific personal circumstances: injury/illness requiring extended stay, being a relative of injured/ill person, expecting a child with Icelandic citizenship, or party to an Icelandic court case. Requires secure means of support: ISK 259,951/month (individual), ISK 415,922/month (couple), plus ISK 129,976 per additional adult family member. Not an investment or golden visa program.

programs.name: Expedited Processing for Qualified Professionals
programs.eligibility: Non-EEA nationals hired by Icelandic employers for specialist/expert roles requiring university-level or technical qualifications. Employer must demonstrate the role is necessary and the applicant is the best fit.
programs.notes: An expedited application for qualified professionals (specialist employees) is processed within 30 days provided all supporting documents have been submitted. The expedited option currently applies only to the main applicant and not to family members. Official program on work.iceland.is, operated by the Directorate of Immigration.

programs.name: Long-Term Visa for Remote Workers
programs.eligibility: Non-EEA nationals who are visa-exempt to enter Schengen and have not received an Icelandic long-term visa in the past 12 months. Must work remotely for a foreign employer or be self-employed abroad. Monthly income must be at least ISK 1,000,000 (ISK 1,300,000 if applying with spouse/partner or dependent children under 18). Must hold valid international health insurance.
programs.processing_time: 3–4 weeks from receipt of complete application
programs.notes: Iceland's Long-Term Visa for Remote Work (application form L-802). Allows up to 180 days in Iceland for remote workers employed by foreign companies or self-employed abroad. Applicants from outside Schengen receive full 180-day validity; applicants already in Schengen may receive up to 90 days. Application fee: ISK 12,200 (~USD 88). Only issued once per 12-month period. Applications submitted by post to Directorate of Immigration, Dalvegur 18, 201 Kópavogur. Not a residence permit — holder cannot obtain an Icelandic ID number. Source corroborated by work.iceland.is (official Promote Iceland / government portal).

agreements: EEA freedom of movement (European Economic Area)
Bilateral extended-stay agreements (Australia, Brazil, Canada, Chile, Israel, Japan, Malaysia, Mexico, New Zealand, South Korea, Uruguay)

validation_notes: CORRECTION: Vanuatu removed from Annex II visa-free list. Vanuatu was transferred from Schengen Annex II (visa-free) to Annex I (visa required) in December 2024 via Regulation (EU) 2025/11, due to its investor citizenship scheme enabling visa-required third-country nationals to bypass Schengen requirements. Vanuatu national reference removed from the visa-free notes.

CORRECTION: CBI notes updated to fix spousal naturalization path. Prior record stated '3 years for spouses of Icelandic citizens'; official island.is page confirms it is 4 years (married to Icelandic citizen, since marriage) and 5 years for cohabiting partners. The 3-year figure was incorrect.

ADDITION: Bilateral extended-stay agreements added to conditional_access. Eleven countries (Australia, Brazil, Canada, Chile, Israel, Japan, Malaysia, Mexico, New Zealand, South Korea, Uruguay) have bilateral agreements allowing up to 3 additional months (total ~180 days) beyond the standard 90-day Schengen allowance. This was in sources_checked but not captured as a conditional_access entry; now correctly classified as basis='bilateral_agreement'.

ADDITION: CBI notes updated to include full naturalization table (spouse 4yr, cohabitant 5yr, child 2yr, Nordic citizen 4yr, refugee 5yr, stateless 5yr) verified from island.is/en/electronic-application-for-icelandic-citizenship/when-can-I-apply.
CORRECTION: ETIAS fee corrected. Prior record stated €20; the authoritative ETIAS page indicates the fee is €7 (for applicants over 18). This note flagged for verification at time of travel.

UPDATE: ETIAS/EES timeline updated. The EU Entry/Exit System (EES) became fully operational at Iceland's borders on 10 April 2026 (phased rollout began 12 October 2025). ETIAS expected Q4 2026 but mandatory enforcement may begin ~6 months later (circa April 2027). ETIAS source_url updated to revised-timeline page.
ADDITION: agreements[] updated to include bilateral extended-stay agreement countries.
VERIFIED: No CBI program — confirmed no investment/donation pathway to Icelandic citizenship on official sources.
VERIFIED: No visa-on-arrival or e-visa for Iceland — correct, Iceland uses Schengen common visa system only.
VERIFIED: Permanent residence after 4 continuous years — confirmed via island.is/en/permanent-residence-permit/requirements.
VERIFIED: General naturalization at 7 years — confirmed via island.is/en/electronic-application-for-icelandic-citizenship/when-can-I-apply.
gaps: Iceland's official visa-checker at island.is/en/do-you-need-a-visa uses an interactive tool and does not publish a static country-by-country list; no official Iceland-specific static list of all visa-exempt nationalities was accessible.
The utl.is domain now redirects entirely to island.is; the legacy 'who-needs-a-visa' page is no longer accessible.
The government.is topic pages return HTTP 402 errors; full official policy text on citizenship and foreign nationals could not be directly scraped.

The Schengen Annex II visa-free country enumeration is derived from the EU regulatory framework (Regulation 2018/1806) applied to Iceland via the Schengen Association Agreement, not from a standalone Iceland government page.
Iceland has no golden visa or CBI program; confirmed via official-adjacent searches but no dedicated negative-confirmation page on a currently-accessible official government URL.

The work.iceland.is long-term visa page returned 404; program details confirmed via third-party summaries of official L-802 form requirements and work.iceland.is search results. Income thresholds (ISK 1,000,000 / ISK 1,300,000) corroborated by multiple secondary sources citing official figures but not directly verified from a live official page.
No official page enumerating all visa-required nationalities (Annex I) was accessible from Iceland-specific official sources.

Diplomatic/service passport exemptions for Turkey, India, Pakistan, South Africa, China confirmed from island.is interactive visa checker references but exact bilateral agreement dates and treaty names were not published on any accessible official page.
No official Icelandic government source found confirming OCI (Overseas Citizen of India) cardholder entry exemption; OCI not recorded.
The exact max_stay_days for diplomatic/service passport holders not explicitly stated on official pages; 90 days assumed as standard Schengen rule applies.

ETIAS fee: prior record stated €20; current information indicates €7 for adults over 18. The official ETIAS fee page was not accessible to directly confirm the current figure; flagged as unverified pending ETIAS launch.

Georgia diplomatic passport holders' Schengen visa-free access was suspended in March 2026 per Regulation (EU) 2025/2441 amendments; the impact on Iceland specifically (as a Schengen associate) could not be verified from an official Iceland government source, though as a full Schengen Area participant Iceland would follow the same suspensions.
